title: The Translation Evaluation Challenge
concept: Translation has many valid answers, so exact matching is a poor metric; we need automated scores that check semantic overlap.

exercises.prompt: Can a single foreign sentence have more than one correct translation in English?

options.text: Yes, there are often many correct ways.
No, there is always exactly one correct translation.
No, translation is impossible for computers.

exercises.explain: Language is flexible. A single sentence can be translated in multiple correct ways using different words and structures.

cards.title: No Single Right Answer
cards.body: If you ask three people to translate a Spanish sentence into English, you might get three different, yet perfectly correct, answers! How do we teach computers to grade translations fairly when there isn't just one right answer? Let's find out!

cards.title: Automated Evaluation
cards.body: Translation evaluation metrics are automated scoring tools that measure how close an AI's translation is to a human-made reference translation. Instead of demanding a perfect word-for-word match, they calculate similarity scores based on shared words and phrases.

cards.title: The Art Judge Analogy
cards.body: Imagine grading an art contest where students paint a 'peaceful morning.' If you only give points to paintings that look exactly like your own, you'd fail dozens of beautiful, correct masterpieces! Translation evaluation is the same: we need a grading system that recognizes different styles of saying the exact same thing.

cards.title: Matching the Meaning
cards.body: If a human translates a sentence to: 'The dog is sleeping,' and the AI outputs: 'The puppy is asleep,' they share almost no exact words, but they mean the same thing. Good evaluation metrics look at synonyms and phrase overlap to award a high similarity score anyway!

cards.title: The Perfect Match Myth
cards.body: Many believe that if an AI translation doesn't match the human template 100%, it must be wrong. In reality, translation is creative! Two completely different sentences can share the exact same meaning, and automated metrics are designed to reward that.

cards.title: Exact vs. Semantic Grading
cards.body: See how rigid matching fails to recognize valid options, while smart semantic overlap metrics successfully grade them.

left.text: Rejects 'It is raining cats and dogs' because the reference says 'It is pouring rain.'

right.text: Grants a high score because both phrases describe heavy rain, capturing the true meaning.

exercises.prompt: Why can't we just use human judges to grade every single AI translation in real-world systems?

options.text: Humans do not know how to read translations.
It is too slow, expensive, and difficult to scale up.
AI translations are always too perfect for humans to understand.
Human judges always agree on everything, which is boring.

exercises.explain: Computers need automated metrics because hiring human bilingual experts to grade millions of sentences is too slow and expensive.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using an exact-match system is a good way to grade translations.
exercises.explain: Because there are many ways to translate a sentence correctly, exact matching would incorrectly mark good translations as wrong.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the rule of translation evaluation.
exercises.sentence: Instead of looking for a perfect word-for-word match, modern metrics measure the overlap in meaning.
exercises.explain: Because translation is flexible, automated scores check if the translated words capture the same overall meaning as the reference.

exercises.prompt: Spot the weak AI model translation of the French phrase 'La voiture rouge roule vite' (correct meaning: 'The red car drives fast'):

options.text: A blue bicycle is parked here.

exercises.explain: The first sentence is a perfect translation that uses different words. The third sentence is completely wrong. The second sentence is generated by a weak AI model that keeps the wrong grammar order.

exercises.prompt: Why is automated translation evaluation designed to look for overlapping meaning rather than an exact word-for-word match?

options.text: It checks if the letters are capitalized correctly.
It allows for multiple valid translations that mean the same thing.
It translates the text back into the original language first.
It requires the AI to use exactly one pre-approved sentence.

exercises.explain: Exact string matching is too strict because a single sentence can be correctly translated in dozens of ways. Modern evaluation metrics focus on measuring semantic overlap and vocabulary similarity instead.
